feed.title: blank
feed.subtitle: A simple, whitespace theme for academics. Based on [*folio](https://github.com/bogoli/-folio) design.

entry.title: My Favorite Derivation of the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality

link.title: My Favorite Derivation of the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality

entry.content:
Let $\mathbf{v,w} \in V$, a real inner product space. Consider the quadratic polynomial defined as the inner product of the vector $\mathbf{v} + t\mathbf{w}$ with itself over $t \in \mathbb{R}$\begin{equation} p(t) = \braket{\mathbf{v} + t\mathbf{w}, \mathbf{v} + t\mathbf{w}}. \end{equation} Since $p(t) \geq 0$, its discriminant must be nonpositive. This yields the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality.

I found this derivation in an excellent book on this and other inequalities titled The Cauchy-Schwarz Master Class: An Introduction to the Art of Mathematical Inequalities by John Michael Steele.

entry.title: Point Mass on a Curve
entry.content:
For my first technical post, I’ll share a problem from classical mechanics that I thought of while sitting in a lecture one day. I caught the physics bug while taking AP Physics 1 (classical mechanics) in junior year of high school, so I thought this would be an appropriate way to start.

A point mass $m$ travels along the smooth function $y(x): \mathbb{R} o \mathbb{R}$, which has kinetic coefficient of friction $\mu$. Find the equation of motion for this particle.

I apologize for the lack of illustration here but I'll do my best to further describe the situation. (I'm very much still learning the theme I'm using for this website; the production value will hopefully get better as time goes on.) I'm imagining a point mass using this curve $y(x)$ as a slide. For instance, take $y(x) = \sin(x) / x$. Then we could imagine placing the particle at, say, the point $(a, \sin(a)/a)$, releasing it, then observing its subsequent motion. Our goal is to find the governing differential equation of this motion. Note that I am assuming the static coefficient of friction is zero. We are also ignoring air resistance.

To solve this problem, we turn to Lagrangian mechanicsIncidentally, for a long time, I was led to believe that Langrangian mechanics can not be applied to systems with nonconservative forces. That is certainly false, as we will see shortly. Incidentally, on a deeper level it is also true that there is no such thing as a nonconservative force. See here: https://www.feynmanlectures.caltech.edu/I_14.html.. This might be opening a can of worms if you have not seen Lagrangian mechanics before. For a good introduction to this topic, see this Feynman lecture. Please note that in this lecture, Feynman does not mention the dissipation function, which we will need here due to the frictional force. Also, I found an extremely helpful forum post which has the answer to my question. However, I feel that this person’s response could be made clearer. There is no mention of the dissipation function or radius of curvature. Even if I do not go into depth on certain topics, I think it is helpful to at least mention them explicitly so that someone who is interested has specific search terms that they can use to look into things further. Apart from that, there is a sign error in an intermediate step (on the potential energy), they are missing an absolute value on one of the terms in the equation of motion, and there is a simplification that can be made for a certain term involving the signum function. That being said, I want to reiterate that this person’s answer was very useful and I was able to use it to check my own final answer.

To make the solution method a little more digestible, let’s begin with the frictionless case. In this case, we only need to take note of the functional known as the “action,” defined by egin{equation} S[q(t)] = \int_{t_0}^{t_1} L(q(t), \dot{q}(t),t)~\mathrm{d}t, \end{equation} where $q(t): \mathbb{R} o \mathbb{R}$Here I'm assuming $q(t): \mathbb{R} \mapsto \mathbb{R}$ because we are only concerned with a single particle's $x$-coordinate (i.e., $q(t) = x(t)$) but we could certainly also have the vectorized version $\vec{q}(t): \mathbb{R} \mapsto \mathbb{R}^n$ to keep track of several particles in multiple dimensions. describes the motion of the particle in generalized coordinates These are the specific set of coordinates used for the problem at hand. We will be using Cartesian coordinates but for a different problem (e.g., those involving a pendulum) polar coordinates may be more appropriate, for example. as a function of time $t$, $L$ is the Lagrangian, and we have used Newton’s dot notation to denote differentiation with respect to time. Further, we are assuming our observation takes place over the time interval $[t_0, t_1]$.

Why do we care about this action thing? Well, whatever mechanism that created the universe that we live in decided that between the points $x(t_0) = x_0$ and $x(t_1) = x_1$, the particle will follow the trajectory $x(t)$ that minimizes the action $S$. Despite having known this for about 8 years now, I still find this principle a bit magical. The principle of least action is equivalent to Newton’s second law, but then you might say it is also not obvious why the second law should be in the form that it is. (Why is it so simple? Why is it a second order differential equation? Why not third order? Fourth? It could have been an integro-differential equation. It could have been nonlinear. The possibilities are endless.) Again, it appears that these are simply the rules of the game that have been laid out, and what’s left for us to do is to play by them, however magical they may seem. We don’t ask why the bishop moves diagonally or why the knight jumps in the shape of an “L”; we play chess!

In classical mechanics, the Lagrangian takes a simple formTo stress the point, let me clearly state that this Lagrangian is specifically for classical mechanics. If we were dealing with electromagnetism for example, the Lagrangian would be something else (albeit similar).: it is the kinetic energy of the particle minus its potential energy. Plugging this in, we have egin{equation} S[x(t)] = \int_{t_0}^{t_1} rac{1}{2} m |\vec{v}|^2 - mgy ~\mathrm{d}t\end{equation} where I have switched from $q(t)$ to $x(t)$ to show that the generalized coordinate I’m using is the position along the $x$-axis in Cartesian coordinates, $\vec{v} \in \mathbb{R}^2$ is the particle velocityTherefore $|\vec{v}|$ is speed., $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity at sea level, and $y$ is the height of the particle as defined in the problem statement. In the interest of cleaner notation, I have suppressed the dependence of $v$ and $y$ on $x(t)$, but this dependence should be obvious from context. From this post onward, you can safely assume I am working in metric units unless I lose my mind and switch to something else, in which case I will point it out. Knowing that we’re using metric units, you can see that the Lagrangian has units of joules and that the action has units of joule-seconds. To minimize the action, we use variational calculus. I can make a post on that if requested.

Variational calculus tells us that the minimizing $x(t)$ should satisfy the Euler-Lagrange equation, egin{equation} \label{EL} rac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}t} rac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}} - rac{\partial L}{\partial x} = 0. \end{equation} As stated, the Lagrangian for our problem is simply the kinetic energy minus the potential energy. The kinetic energy is egin{align} rac{1}{2} m |\vec{v}|^2 &= rac{1}{2} m (v_x^2 + v_y^2), \end{align} where $v_x$ is the velocity in the $x$-direction and $v_y$ is the velocity in the $y$-direction. Since $x(t)$ is the $x$-coordinate of the position of the particle, $v_x$ is simply $\dot{x}(t)$. Similarly, $v_y$ is $\dot{y}(t)$, which we can write in terms of $\dot{x}(t)$ by using the chain rule, yielding $\dot{y}(t) = rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}\dot{x}(t)$. Therefore, egin{align} \vec{v} &= \dot{x} egin{bmatrix} 1 ewline rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} \end{bmatrix} ewline \implies |\vec{v}|^2 &= \dot{x}^2\left[1 + \left( rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} ight)^2 ight] \end{align}

Meanwhile, the potential energy is $mgy$. Therefore, the Lagrangian is egin{equation} \label{lagrangian} L = rac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2\left[1 + \left( rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} ight)^2 ight] - mgy.\end{equation} Now we compute the different terms in the Euler-Lagrange equation: egin{align} rac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}} &= m \dot{x}\left[1 + \left( rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} ight)^2 ight] ewline \implies rac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}t} rac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}} &= m \ddot{x}\left[1 + \left( rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} ight)^2 ight] + 2m\dot{x}^2 rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} rac{\mathrm{d}^2y}{\mathrm{d}x^2}\end{align} and egin{equation} rac{\partial L}{\partial x} = m \dot{x}^2 rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} rac{\mathrm{d}^2y}{\mathrm{d}x^2} - mg rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}. \end{equation} Plugging into \eqref{EL} and rearranging, we have

egin{equation} \ddot{x} = - rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} rac{g + \dot{x}^2 rac{\mathrm{d}^2y}{\mathrm{d}x^2}}{1 + \left( rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} ight)^2}. \label{frictionless} \end{equation}

Awesome! We now have a compact description of how a particle would traverse the frictionless curve $y(x)$. You might say that the position of the particle as a function of time is described by $x(t)$ (and $y(x(t))$) which is not what we have here. That may be true, but we could solve this equation numerically for $x(t)$. I used the $ exttt{odeint}$ function in SciPy (a Python library) to create the figures for this post. See Fig BLAAAAHHHHH for a point particle traversing a frictionless parabola.

Only for a very simple curve $y(x)$ could we ever hope to get a closed form solution for $x(t)$. For $y(x) = x$, $\dot{x}(0) = 0$, $x(0) = 0$, we obtain $x(t) = -gt^2/4$ (and therefore $y(t) = -gt^2/4$). Let’s do a couple of sanity checks. For one, these expressions have the correct units of meters, since $g$ has units of meters per seconds squared and $t^2$ has units of seconds squared, giving meters once multiplied together. Also, as $t$ increases, notice that $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ become increasingly negative. That’s exactly what we should expect if we place the particle at the origin on the curve $y(x) = x$. It should slide down and to the left, which is exactly what our expressions for $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ are telling us. The velocity of the particle is also becoming increasingly negative in both components which is also to be expected since the particle is under the influence of gravity. One interesting feature of \eqref{frictionless} is that it is independent of the particle’s mass.

Now let’s return to the original problem, which involves friction. To handle that, we need to modify \eqref{EL}. Allow me to pull a couple more fast ones on you (the first one was the concept of “action”). First, we need to insert a “generalized force” on the right hand side and second, we will need to find a coefficient of this generalized force. A generalized force is one that is defined over generalized coordinates. We have already specified that we will be working with the coordinate $x(t)$ here and so the generalized force we will add is in terms of this. It might feel pedantic that I’m calling it a “generalized force” and not just a “force” but I want to use the proper terms so that you will have some familiarity with them already if you decide to read further and come across them. Otherwise, they might seem rather alien and you might feel cheated by my current usage of Lagrangian mechanics.

As stated, let’s add a generalized force on the right hand side of \eqref{EL} egin{equation} \label{modified} rac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}t} rac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}} - rac{\partial L}{\partial x} = Q, \end{equation} where egin{equation} Q = - rac{\partial D}{\partial \dot{x}},~ D = rac{1}{N_v+1}\sum_{k} c_k |\vec{v_k}|^{N_v+1} \end{equation} are the generalized force and dissipation function, respectively. This is the modification that will allow us to handle dissipative forces in the Langrangian formalism. The sum is over the $k$ objects in a system so for this problem, we will only retain one term. Further, $N_v$ is the order of the velocity dependence for the friction we are considering. In our case, we are not considering velocity-dependent friction the way we would have to if we were considering air resistance, for example. Therefore, $N_v=0$. We already found $|\vec{v}|$ (I’m suppressing the subscript because we only have one object) earlier and used it in \eqref{lagrangian}. Using that same $|\vec{v}|$, we find $D = c\dot{x}\sqrt{1 + ( rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x})^2}$. Plugging this into \eqref{modified}, we have egin{equation} \label{modified2} rac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}t} rac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}} - rac{\partial L}{\partial x} = -c\sqrt{1 + \left( rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} ight)^2}. \end{equation} Let’s do some dimensional analysis. On the left hand side, we have the term $ rac{\partial L}{\partial x}$. As stated earlier, the Lagrangian has units of joules. Therefore, this term has units of joules per meter, or newtons. That implies the right hand side must also have units of newtons and they must be contained in the $c$ term, since $\sqrt{1 + ( rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x})^2}$ is unitless. This is the third and final fast one I will pull: $c$ is the force of friction. Of course, friction had to show up somewhere and $c$ has units of force so it feels morally correct to put them together but it is reasonable to question why $c$ is exactly equal to friction. It could have been some dimensionless constant times the force of friction, where that constant is not equal to one. Yet, that is not the case. At this point, I have to confess my ignorance. Please enlighten me if you have an explanation for this. Allow me to set $c = F_{\mu}$ to denote this relationship and move onward.

Friction is proportional to the normal force, where the proportionality constant is the friction coefficient, i.e., $F_{\mu} = \mu N$. So now the task is to determine $N$. To do this, we need to recall the topic of uniform circular motion: when an object is constrained to move on a circular path at constant speed. Let’s pretend for a moment that our particle is undergoing uniform circular motion. Then, a free body diagram would reveal the relationship egin{equation} \label{normal} N - (mg)_{\perp} = rac{m|\vec{v}|^2}{r}, \end{equation} where $(mg)_{\perp}$ is the component of gravity perpendicular to the circular path and pointing away from the center of the circle and $r$ is the radius. Some simple trigonometry reveals that $(mg)_{\perp} = mg\cos( heta)$, where $ heta$ is the angle the particle makes with the horizontal. I again apologize for the lack of illustration here but I’m hoping that you might recall or be able to quickly rederive this trigonometric fact. It is exactly the same angle $ heta$ you would find in a standard inclined plane problem. Another trigonometric fact we need is that $ an( heta)$ is exactly equal to $ rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}$. This holds for any reasonable $y(x)$, not just a circular arc. Therefore, after writing $\cos( heta)$ in terms of $ an( heta)$, we have egin{equation} \cos( heta) = rac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \left( rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} ight)^2}}. \end{equation} That takes care of $(mg)_{\perp}$, and we would have everything we need to find the normal force if the particle was moving along a circular trajectory with radius $r$. However, we want an expression for the normal force (and therefore friction) to hold for a more general $y(x)$, not just one that traces out a circular arc. For a more general $y(x)$, we can treat the curve locally as part of a circular arc. In other words, if we zoom in enough, the particle appears to be traversing along a circle and from one point to the next along $y(x)$, it is traversing along circles of different radii. As an extreme example, even if the particle were traveling in a straight line, we could still treat it as traversing on a circular arc; one with radius equal to infinity. Hopefully, this makes some sense intuitively but is there a way to properly quantify the radius associated with each point of $y(x)$? The answer is yes (or else we would be in trouble and I would have to end this post here)! Borrowing from differential geometry, the radius we want is egin{equation} r = rac{(1 + ( rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x})^2)^{ rac{3}{2}}}{ rac{\mathrm{d}^2y}{\mathrm{d}x^2}}. \end{equation} More precisely, this is known as the “radius of curvature.” And with that, we have everything we need to find the normal force. Rearranging \eqref{normal} and plugging in what we just found, we have egin{align} N = m rac{g + \dot{x}^2 rac{\mathrm{d}^2y}{\mathrm{d}x^2}}{\sqrt{1 + \left( rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} ight)^2}}.\end{align} A subtlety we need to account for is the sign on friction. We need to ensure that friction is always opposing the particle motion. If the particle is moving to the right, friction should be to the left and vice versa. To account for this, we tack on a factor of $\mathrm{sgn}(\dot{x})$ to $N$.

We now have everything we need to plug into \eqref{modified2}, which yields egin{align} m\ddot{x}\left[1 + \left( rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} ight)^2 ight] + m\dot{x}^2 rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} rac{\mathrm{d}^2y}{\mathrm{d}x^2} + mg rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} &= -\mu N \mathrm{sgn}(\dot{x})\sqrt{1+\left( rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} ight)^2} ewline &= -\mu m \mathrm{sgn}(\dot{x})\left(g + \dot{x}^2 rac{\mathrm{d}^2y}{\mathrm{d}x^2} ight).\end{align} After rearranging, we have

egin{equation} \ddot{x} = - rac{ \left(g + \dot{x}^2 rac{\mathrm{d}^2y}{\mathrm{d}x^2} ight) \left( rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} + \mu\,\mathrm{sgn}(\dot{x}) ight) }{ 1 + \left( rac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} ight)^2 }. \label{frictionEOM} \end{equation}

Nice! We now have a compact description of the particle motion for the more difficult case where we take friction into account. If we plug in $\mu = 0$, we get back to the frictionless equation of motion given by \eqref{frictionless}, as we should. As in the frictionless case, \eqref{frictionEOM} is also independent of the particle’s mass.

A simple, elegant caption looks good between image rows, after each row, or doesn't have to be there at all.

entry.title: What this is and what this isn’t

link.title: What this is and what this isn’t

entry.content:
In short, this is what it is and it’s not what it’s not. In other words, I don’t want to pigeonhole this into a specific category, although it may end up following some common theme(s). I don’t know. We’ll have to wait and see.

For now, I can tell you what I currently want to write about. I would like to share things from physics and math which I find interesting and/or useful. This will probably end up being a mix of elementary and advanced topics, but of course the difficulty level is subjective.

I would be really happy if someone found some entertainment and/or learned something new here but I also have selfish reasons for writing: this will help me organize my thoughts on certain ideas and force me to be clear and correct with the ultimate goal of clearing up any confusions I may have and having references I can return to in the future. Terence Tao has a great post on this that resonates with me.

I expect that some posts will be more useful as references and others will prioritize pedagogy. Also, I might do this for 2 weeks then abandon it. Or maybe I’ll post a bunch in the first few months then post once a year. Again, we’ll see how it goes.

I should make a note on rigor. You should expect rigor in the flavor of a physicist or engineer rather than a mathematician. I want to be able to use the Dirac delta without invoking Schwartz’ work that put it on rigorous grounding; if it was good enough for Dirac to use before Schwartz, it’s good enough for me. I want to be able to exchange integrals and derivatives without anxiety. I do not want to spend time proving the conditions for the dominated convergence theorem hold before switching limits and integrals—I’m sorry, but I have a finite lifespan. Maybe every now and then I’ll throw in something like that but yes, some combination of me finding it unnecessary, boring, or overly time-consuming will prevent those kinds of arguments appearing here for the most part. I’ll share a relevant quote from the preface of Anthony Zee’s quantum field theory book:

Indeed, a Fields Medalist once told me that top mathematicians secretly think like physicists and after they work out the broad outline of a proof they then dress it up with epsilons and deltas [...] As we like to say in the theoretical physics community, too much rigor soon leads to rigor mortis. Be warned.

— Anthony Zee, Quantum Field Theory in a Nutshell, 2nd Edition

Having said that, I will strive to not be egregiously hand-wavy either. For any steps that I am not completely comfortable with, I will be honest and point those out as they arise instead of pretending hand-waviness makes sense. Consider those occurrences as invitations to enlighten me.

Anyway, as I said at the start, I want to be able to talk about a variety of topics here. So even if I begin with a bunch of physics and math, do not be surprised by movie recs, rants on American media and car centricity, showerthoughts, etc..

entry.title: The Three-Body Problem
entry.content:
He remembered taking a class in information theory as a third-year student in college. The professor had put up two pictures: One was the famous Song Dynasty painting Along the River During the Qingming Festival, full of fine, rich details; the other was a photograph of the sky on a sunny day, the deep blue expanse broken only by a wisp of cloud that one couldn’t even be sure was there. The professor asked the class which picture contained more information. The answer was that the photograph’s information content—its entropy—exceeded the painting’s by one or two orders of magnitude.

Three Body was the same. Its enormous information content was hidden deep. Wang could feel it, but he could not articulate it. He suddenly understood that the makers of Three Body took the exact opposite of the approach taken by designers of other games. Normally, game designers tried to display as much information as possible to increase the sense of realism. But Three Body’s designers worked to compress the information content to disguise a more complex reality, just like that seemingly empty photograph of the sky.

— Liú Cíxīn, The Three-Body Problem
